title: Topic 228: Why Players Mute Game Audio: The Podcast Gaming Phenomenon
content:
# Topic 228: Why Players Mute Game Audio: The Podcast Gaming Phenomenon

## Abstract

This paper delves into the burgeoning phenomenon of gamers muting in-game audio to consume extraneous auditory content, specifically podcasts. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this seemingly counter-intuitive behavior, which we term 'Podcast Gaming.' While conventional wisdom suggests that dividing attention compromises performance, our research indicates that for many, this form of multitasking enhances the gaming experience, transforming monotonous grinding into an opportunity for intellectual or comedic enrichment. This study synthesizes existing literature on multitasking and cognitive load with anecdotal evidence from gaming communities to argue that Podcast Gaming is not merely a distraction, but a strategic enhancement of leisure time, reflecting a broader trend of hyper-efficient content consumption in modern society.

## Introduction

In the hallowed halls of gaming, a silent rebellion is brewing. A rebellion not against oppressive in-game regimes or poorly designed quests, but against the very soundscapes meticulously crafted by developers. We are, of course, talking about the growing legion of players who, with a single click, banish the epic orchestral scores and visceral sound effects to the digital ether, replacing them with the soothing tones of a true-crime podcast or the boisterous laughter of their favorite comedians. This is the world of 'Podcast Gaming,' a phenomenon that, at first glance, seems as logical as reading a book while watching a movie.

For decades, game audio has been considered an integral part of the immersive experience. It warns us of impending doom, celebrates our victories, and sets the emotional tone for our virtual adventures. Yet, a significant portion of the gaming populace is willingly sacrificing this crucial sensory input. Why? Are they auditory masochists? Or is there a deeper, more profound psychological driver at play? This paper posits that the latter is true. We will explore how the modern gamer, a master of efficiency and a connoisseur of content, is optimizing their leisure time by layering auditory information over their interactive entertainment. This is not a rejection of the game, but an evolution of its consumption. We will dissect the motivations, from the desire to make 'grinding' more palatable to the simple human need for a good story, and in doing so, uncover a fascinating intersection of psychology, technology, and consumer behavior.

## Literature Review

The academic world has, in its infinite wisdom, been slow to address the podcast gaming phenomenon directly. However, a wealth of research on multitasking and cognitive load provides a fertile ground for our investigation. Early studies on multitasking, such as those by Broadbent (1958), presented a bottleneck model of attention, suggesting that our cognitive resources are limited and that attempting to perform two tasks at once will inevitably lead to a decline in performance in at least one of them. This aligns with the common-sense notion that a gamer listening to a podcast would be less responsive to in-game cues. Indeed, a study by Cardoso-Leite et al. (2016) found that heavy media multitaskers performed worse on a variety of cognitive tasks, suggesting that our brains are not, in fact, wired to handle multiple streams of information flawlessly [2].

However, more recent research has begun to challenge this monolithic view of multitasking. The Uses and Gratifications (U&G) theory, traditionally applied to media consumption, suggests that individuals actively choose media to satisfy their specific needs (Katz, Blumler, & Gurevitch, 1973). A 2025 study by Bae, Cho, and Yoo on multitasking in gaming applied this theory to find that multitasking can actually *increase* cognitive and emotional satisfaction, leading to a higher intention for continued game use [1]. This suggests that gamers are not simply distracting themselves, but are actively curating their experience to maximize their enjoyment. This is particularly relevant in the context of 'grinding' – the repetitive, often tedious tasks required to progress in many games. The podcast, in this scenario, becomes a tool for transforming a chore into a more pleasurable activity.

Furthermore, the concept of 'cognitive load' is crucial to understanding this phenomenon. Sweller's (1988) Cognitive Load Theory posits that our working memory has a limited capacity. When a task does not fully occupy this capacity, there is spare 'cognitive slack' that can be allocated to a secondary task. Many gaming activities, particularly grinding, are low in cognitive load. They are repetitive, require little strategic thought, and rely more on muscle memory than active problem-solving. In these situations, listening to a podcast does not overload the cognitive system, but rather fills the unused capacity, preventing boredom and maintaining engagement.

## Analysis

The act of podcast gaming can be dissected through several psychological lenses. At its core, it is a testament to the brain's remarkable ability to automate tasks and seek out novel stimuli. The modern gamer is, in many ways, a highly trained cognitive athlete, capable of executing complex in-game maneuvers with minimal conscious thought. This is the principle of automaticity in action. As players master the mechanics of a game, the cognitive load required to perform basic actions decreases, freeing up mental resources for other tasks. This is why a seasoned World of Warcraft player can navigate treacherous dungeons while simultaneously absorbing the intricacies of the Peloponnesian War via a history podcast. Their hands are on autopilot, their minds are free to roam.

This leads us to the concept of 'flow state,' a term coined by Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi (1990). Flow is a state of complete immersion in an activity, characterized by a feeling of energized focus, full involvement, and enjoyment in the process of the activity. While it may seem that adding a podcast would disrupt this state, for many gamers, it can actually facilitate it. The podcast provides a steady stream of auditory stimulation that can help to drown out distractions and maintain a consistent level of engagement, particularly during the more monotonous segments of a game. It's a form of self-administered cognitive scaffolding, a way to keep the mind occupied just enough to prevent it from wandering, but not so much that it interferes with the primary task.

However, the plot thickens when we consider the potential downsides. As the study by Cardoso-Leite et al. (2016) suggests, not all multitasking is created equal [2]. The type of game and the nature of the podcast are crucial variables. A player attempting to follow the intricate plot of a story-heavy RPG while listening to a dense philosophy podcast is likely to experience cognitive overload, resulting in a diminished experience on both fronts. The sweet spot for podcast gaming seems to lie in the combination of a low-cognitive-load game and a high-interest, passively consumed podcast. This is the digital equivalent of knitting while watching television, a time-honored tradition of productive distraction.

Furthermore, we must consider the role of personality. The 'sensation-seeking' personality trait, characterized by a need for novel and intense stimulation, may be a significant predictor of who is more likely to engage in podcast gaming. For these individuals, the game alone may not provide enough stimulation, and the addition of a podcast serves to satisfy their craving for a richer sensory experience. In a world saturated with content, the act of consuming one stream of media at a time can feel inefficient, almost wasteful. Podcast gaming is the ultimate expression of this 'content maximalist' mindset, a desire to wring every last drop of entertainment and information from every waking moment.

## Real-World Examples

The podcast gaming phenomenon is not a theoretical construct; it is a lived reality for millions of players. The internet is rife with anecdotal evidence, from Reddit threads to Discord servers, where gamers share their favorite game-podcast pairings. One of the most commonly cited examples is the combination of 'grindy' games like *Old School RuneScape* or *Warframe* with long-form conversational podcasts like 'The Joe Rogan Experience' or 'My Favorite Murder.' In these cases, the repetitive nature of the gameplay loop, which often involves hours of clicking on rocks or shooting the same enemies, becomes a meditative backdrop for the engaging narratives of the podcast.

A particularly illustrative case can be found in the world of professional gaming. In a Reddit thread discussing the practice of pro *Valorant* players muting their game audio after death, it was revealed that many do so to give their brains a 'rest' from the intense auditory stimulation of the game [3]. While not directly related to podcast consumption, this highlights the cognitive fatigue that can result from prolonged exposure to game audio and the inherent need for auditory variety. It is a small leap to imagine these same players filling the silence with a podcast during their downtime.

Streamers, the public-facing ambassadors of gaming culture, also provide a fascinating case study. Many popular streamers, such as 'xQc' and 'Asmongold,' are known to play games with muted or low-volume audio while interacting with their chat or watching other videos. This is a form of multitasking born out of the unique demands of their profession, where the game is often secondary to the performance of being an entertainer. The game becomes a visual anchor, a canvas upon which they paint their personality, while the primary source of engagement comes from their interaction with their audience. This is podcast gaming in reverse, where the streamer *is* the podcast, and the game is the passive secondary activity.

Finally, we have the rise of 'idle games,' a genre of games that are designed to be played in the background with minimal player input. Games like *Cookie Clicker* and *AdVenture Capitalist* are the ultimate expression of low-cognitive-load gaming, and they are practically begging to be paired with a podcast. These games provide a sense of progression and accomplishment without demanding the player's full attention, making them the perfect companion for a long commute or a lazy Sunday afternoon of podcast binging.

## Discussion

The podcast gaming phenomenon offers a fascinating window into the evolving landscape of media consumption and human behavior. It challenges the traditional notion of what it means to be ‘immersed’ in a game, suggesting that for many, immersion is not a singular, all-encompassing state, but a customizable experience that can be augmented with external stimuli. This has significant implications for game developers, who have historically poured vast resources into creating rich, cinematic soundscapes. While these are undoubtedly valuable for story-driven, high-immersion games, the rise of podcast gaming suggests that for a significant portion of the player base, the audio is, at best, a secondary concern, and at worst, an annoyance to be muted.

This is not to say that game audio is dead. Rather, it suggests a need for a more nuanced approach to audio design. Perhaps future games could offer more customizable audio options, allowing players to toggle specific sound elements on and off, or even integrate their own music and podcasts directly into the game client. The success of games with minimalist or procedural soundtracks, such as *Minecraft*, further supports the idea that not every game needs a bombastic, Hollywood-style score to be successful. Sometimes, the most effective soundtrack is the one the player brings with them.

From a consumer behavior perspective, podcast gaming is a prime example of the ‘attention economy’ in action. In a world where we are constantly bombarded with content, our attention has become a scarce and valuable resource. Podcast gaming is a way of maximizing the return on our attention investment, a way of squeezing every last drop of productivity and entertainment out of our leisure time. It is a behavior that is both deeply modern and profoundly human, a testament to our innate desire to learn, to be entertained, and to make the most of the time we have. It is, in short, the ultimate life hack for the digital age.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, the act of muting game audio to listen to podcasts is not an act of heresy, but a rational, and even optimal, response to the modern media landscape. It is a declaration of independence from the tyranny of the pre-packaged experience, a testament to the gamer's ingenuity in crafting their own personalized leisure. We have seen how this behavior is rooted in established psychological principles, from the automation of low-cognitive-load tasks to the pursuit of a flow state. We have explored its real-world manifestations, from the grind-heavy landscapes of MMORPGs to the high-stakes world of professional esports. The podcast gaming phenomenon is more than just a quirk; it is a sign of the times, a reflection of our insatiable appetite for content and our relentless drive to optimize our lives, even in our moments of play. So, the next time you see a gamer with their headphones on but the game on mute, do not judge them. They are not missing out. They are leveling up, in more ways than one.
references:
[1] Bae, Y., Cho, J. H., & Yoo, C. (2025). Why gamers use multitasking and how it works on satisfaction and reuse intention. *Entertainment Computing*, *53*, 100935. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.entcom.2025.100935

[2] Cardoso-Leite, P., Kludt, R., Vignola, G., Ma, W. J., Green, C. S., & Bavelier, D. (2016). Technology consumption and cognitive control: Contrasting action video game experience with media multitasking. *Psychonomic bulletin & review*, *23*(5), 1568–1574. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-015-0988-0

[3] Aabed_nerd. (2022, July 19). *About players muting game audio to listen to enemy team comms* [Online forum post]. Reddit. https://www.reddit.com/r/ValorantCompetitive/comments/w2t7oa/about_players_muting_game_audio_to_listen_to/
